coredata.dc:title: Novel highly selective fluorescence sensing strategy for Mercury(Ⅱ) in water based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Guiqiao
Zhang, Shurong
Cui, Jinzhi
Gao, Wensu
Rong, Xing
Lu, Yaxin
Gao, Canzhu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a fluorescent signal-closing probe of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) was developed for quantitative detection of mercury ions (Hg2+). In this detection system, the NCQDs with high quantum yield (QY, 63.80 %) were synthesized via simple hydrothermal method with Methyl Glycine Diacetic acid Trisodium Salt (MGDA) and m-phenylenediamine (MPD) as carbon and nitrogen sources. The NCQDs have a typical surface structure and exceptional fluorescence stability, and their fluorescence zones are centered on excitation wavelengths of 440 nm and emission wavelengths of 510 nm. Under optimal conditions, the NCQDs have outstanding anti-interference ability to various ions and high selectivity to mercury ions. The fluorescence intensity of the detection system is weakened due to the generation of non-fluorescent groups caused by the static quenching effect. The fluorescence quenching efficiency shows a fascinating linear relationship with Hg2+ ions at 0–100 μM (y = 0.0051x-0.015, R2 = 0.9943), and the detection limit is 0.9 μM. Acute toxicity test shows that NCQDs have low toxicity and little harm to environment. The detection system can be used for the quantification of mercury ions in environmental water samples, and the recovery rate is between 99.64 % and 103.43 %, indicating that it is a simple and economical fluorescence detection method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Mercury ions
Fluorescence quenching
Acute toxicity

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of NCQDs to Hg2+
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of NCQDs by Hg2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Thanks for the support of experimental Instrument Platform of School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of NCQDs. (b) Size distribution of NCQDs. (c) XRD patterns of NCQDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of NCQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of NCQDs and high-resolution spectrum of C1s (b), N1s (c), and O1s (d) of the NCQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption of NCQDs. Inset: the pictures of NCQDs under the sunlight and UV light (365 nm); (b) 3D-EEM spectra for NCQDs; The relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of N-CQDs in different pH (c), NaCl salt solutions (d), UV irradiation time (e) and dark placement time (f).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of NCQDs in various concentrations of Hg2+. (b) Relationship between the fluorescence quenching rate and the concentration of Hg2+ (0–500 μM); inset: linear fitting diagram for mercury detection (0–100 μM). (c) NCQDs and NCQDs + Hg2+ relative fluorescence intensities when exposed to various ions.
(a) Fluorescence decay traces of NCQDs and NCQDs + Hg2+. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Hg2+, NCQDs and NCQDs + Hg2+. (c) The 13C NMR spectra of NCQDs and NCQDs with addition of Hg2+ ions.
Comparison of the performance of different fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection.
Quantitative determination of Hg2+ in actual water samples.

head.ce:title: Novel highly selective fluorescence sensing strategy for Mercury(Ⅱ) in water based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Qingdao, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Qingdao, Shandong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a fluorescent signal-closing probe of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) was developed for quantitative detection of mercury ions (Hg2+). In this detection system, the NCQDs with high quantum yield (QY, 63.80 %) were synthesized via simple hydrothermal method with Methyl Glycine Diacetic acid Trisodium Salt (MGDA) and m-phenylenediamine (MPD) as carbon and nitrogen sources. The NCQDs have a typical surface structure and exceptional fluorescence stability, and their fluorescence zones are centered on excitation wavelengths of 440 nm and emission wavelengths of 510 nm. Under optimal conditions, the NCQDs have outstanding anti-interference ability to various ions and high selectivity to mercury ions. The fluorescence intensity of the detection system is weakened due to the generation of non-fluorescent groups caused by the static quenching effect. The fluorescence quenching efficiency shows a fascinating linear relationship with Hg2+ ions at 0–100 μM (y = 0.0051x-0.015, R2 = 0.9943), and the detection limit is 0.9 μM. Acute toxicity test shows that NCQDs have low toxicity and little harm to environment. The detection system can be used for the quantification of mercury ions in environmental water samples, and the recovery rate is between 99.64 % and 103.43 %, indicating that it is a simple and economical fluorescence detection method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution is one of the prominent and most difficult issues facing the world today [1]. Among them, mercury pollution has been concerned by many scholars. Mercury ions are highly toxic, carcinogenic, teratogenic and mutagenic, causing serious threats to human health, severely damaging endocrine, digestive system, nervous system functions, resulting in Minamata disease and Alzheimer's disease [2,3]. Mercury(II), often known as Hg2+, is one of the many forms and is considered to be the most dangerous and stable inorganic form, especially in aquatic environments [4]. Therefore, how to efficiently and quickly detect mercury concentration in water is a key problem to be solved [5,6]. Many methods have been used to detect Hg2+ ions include inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7,8], atomic absorption/emission spectrometry (AAS/AES) [9,10], X-ray fluorescence spectrometry (XRF) [11], etc. These methods, while relatively sensitive, require complex pretreatment schemes and sophisticated instruments [12], resulting in significantly higher testing costs and limiting the development of mercury ions detection in conventional environments [13]. In recent years, fluorescence detection and analysis method has gained great attention because of its high sensitivity, simple operation, low cost, and meet the requirements of ion detection in a variety of scenarios [14–16].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a revolutionary small-sized carbon nanomaterial with fascinating water solubility, low toxicity and optical stability [17–20]. At present, the researches on CQDs are very popular. Researchers use CQDs to change the wavelength of light to improve the efficiency of photocatalysis [21–23], and effectively promote the degradation of a variety of pollutants. Other researchers have used the biocompatibility and fluorescence properties of CQDs for biomarkers and cellular imaging, such as for assessing intracellular pH levels and for imaging fluorescence in mice [24,25]. The application of CQDs in ion detection is also a focus of research. Researchers use the fluorescence change characteristics of CQDs on a certain ion to quantitatively detect ion concentration [26]. However, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of most CQDs is low, which makes it difficult to realize the efficient utilization of raw materials. Therefore, QY has become major concerns in the field of chemical sensing applications for carbon quantum dots [11,27]. It is reported that by doping various non-metallic elements such as nitrogen [28,29], sulfur [30] and boron [31], the surface state and electron distribution can be changed, and the QY of the products can be significantly improved [32,33]. Although the exact synthesis mechanism remains to be studied, it is reasonable to believe that nitrogen-doped CQDs can be of great interest for promoting fluorescence quantum yield and chemical sensing applications.

In recent years, reports on the detection of mercury ions using element-doped carbon quantum dots have gradually increased. The nitrogen or sulfur doped carbon quantum dots synthesized [34,35] by microwave method can be used as a tool for quantitative detection of Hg2+ ions, but the fluorescence quantum yields of their products were only 26 % and 3.5 %, indicating a low utilization rate of light quantum. Moreover, these reports lack studies on biological toxicity and ignore the impact of products on the environment, which is not convincing for the sustainable development of CQDs. Therefore, it is necessary to pay more attention not only to the efficient optical characteristics and ion detection ability of carbon quantum dots, but also to the environmental effects of the products.

In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were successfully fabricated by one-step hydrothermal treatment using Methyl Glycine Diacetic acid Trisodium Salt (MGDA) and m-phenylenediamine (MPD). The objectives of the project were (1) to explore the synthesis scheme of NCQDs; (2) to evaluate the reliability and sensitivity of NCQDs for detection of mercury ions, including the calculation of detection limits, the influence of interfering ions, and the application in natural water bodies; (3) to explore the principle of mercury ion detection method; (4) to assess the sensing system's impact on the toxicity of aquatic organisms.

MGDA (purity = 40 %) was supplied by Shandong Taihe Water Treatment Technologies Co., ltd. (Shandong, China). MPD (purity = 99 %), NaOH, H2SO4, HNO3, HCl, NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, BaCl2·2H2O, NH4Cl, H2SO4, HgSO4, NaHCO3, Na2CO3, H3PO4, K2Cr2O7 and H3BO3 were provided from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). AgNO3, AlCl3, ZnCl2, CuCl2·2H2O, MnSO4·H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CdCl2∙1/2H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, Na2SO3, CH3COOH, CH3CH2OH and Rhodamine 6G were provided from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Unless otherwise stated, the above reagents are analytically pure and have not been further purified. Ultrapure water was used for all tests and preparation of solutions.

The morphological characteristics of NCQDs were obtained by transmission electron microscope (TEM) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV (JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan). And the particle size distribution was obtained by image J software. Thermo K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (America) was utilized to study the X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectra to determine the chemical composition of NCQDs (the operating voltage was 14.6 kV, and the filament current was 13.5 mA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of NCQDs was obtained on an Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffraction system (Japan) with a Cu Kα radiation by scanning from 10° to 80° at the scan rate of 10° per min. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were performed using Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 (America) to confirm the synthesis of NCQDs and the FT-IR was performed in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectrometer was used to obtain the 1H NMR spectra (400Mhz) and 13C NMR spectra (100Mhz) with the deuterated solvent was D2O (Bruker Avance NEO 600 M, Switzerland). The fluorescence lifetime was obtained by EI fls1000 with an excitation source of Nano LEDs (Edinburgh Instruments, Britain). The Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra and the fluorescence spectra of NCQDs were obtained on UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan, slit width was 2 nm) and Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan), respectively. The F-4600 characteristics were set as: the photomultiplier tube voltage was 700 V, the excitation bandwidth was 5 nm and the mission bandwidth was 5 nm, the scanning speed was 1200 nm min−1.

The scheme design is carried out with reference to the existing hydrothermal method [32,36]. NCQDs with a molar ratio of MGDA to MPD of 1:4 was prepared. MGDA (0.72 mmol) and MPD (2.88 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water and then 900 μL of HCl (3.0 M) was added into the system. After mixing well, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for 4 h of hydrothermal treatment at 200℃. The resulting solution was cooled naturally to 22℃ (room temperature) and then passed through a 0.22 μm filter (water system membrane). The filtrate was dialyzed for 24 h in pure water (Molecular Weight Cut Off, MWCO: 500 Da). Lastly, the dialysate was dried to obtain NCQDs powder.

The relative QY was determined as previously established method [37]. Rhodamine 6G dissolved in ethanol (QY = 95 %) was chosen as the reference and the formula is as follows:
(1)
QY
N
=
QY
R
×
( F
N
/
F R
) ×
( A
R
/
A N
)
×
(
η
N
2
/
η
R
2
)
where the subscripts ‘‘N’’ and ‘‘R’’ refer to NCQDs and Rhodamine 6G solution, respectively. QY is the quantum yield, F is the integral area of fluorescence intensity at 440 nm excitation wavelength, A represents the absorbance value, and η is the refractive index of the solvent (ηN = 1.33, ηR = 1.36). Always keep the absorbance value below 0.05 to reduce the interference of absorption effect.

The NCQDs were diluted by Britton-Robinson (B-R) buffer solution to obtain a reserve solution (5 mg/L). The starting solutions (0.1 M) of various ions were prepared, including Mn2+, Al3+, Cr6+, Ag+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, NH4 +, Na+, Hg2+, Cl-, Br-, I-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, SO4 2-, PO4 3- and SO3 2-. The same concentration of ions was added into the N-CQDs solution, only mercury can produce obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon. Finally, in order to investigate the selectivity of the NCQDs to Hg2+ ions, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the mixture with co-existing interferences were measured.

Shandong University (Qingdao, Shandong Province) provided the water samples including tap water and river water. To remove insoluble impurities, all samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm water system membrane and diluted with B-R buffer solution to maintain acidity balance. Then added a quantitative Hg2+ in the water samples and executed fluorescence test for verification [38].

The toxicity test of NCQDs was adopted by ISO 7346-1:1996 and GB/T 13267-1991 (China). Zebrafishes were purchased from Shandong YiXiYue Biotechnology Co., ltd. The zebrafishes were domesticated at room temperature for two weeks. Feeding was stopped 24 h before the experiment, and no food was added during the experiment. Configured different concentrations of NCQDs, and set a group of blank experiments. Ten zebrafishes were randomly placed into each solution to observe the survival of 24 h, 48 h, 72 h and 96 h [39,40].

In this study, the effects of reaction temperature, time and raw reactants ratio on the fluorescence characteristics of NCQDs were discussed (Fig. S1). Temperature is the main factor affecting the carbonization process of precursor. So, the optimal synthesis temperature was set at 200℃. Too low or too high temperature is not suitable to the synthesis process of carbon quantum dots. The effect of time on the synthesis process is not obvious, so 4 h was selected as the synthesis time to ensure green and efficient preparation conditions. Moreover, the change of precursor ratio greatly affects the fluorescence characteristics of the product, which was speculated to be related to the changes of chromophore in the finished product of NCQDs. The optimal preparation of NCQDs was designed ultimately wherein the molar ratio of MGDA and MPD was 1:4 at 200 ℃ for 4 h. The QY of NCQDs is 63.80 % under the best preparation conditions (Eq. (1)), which had excellent fluorescence performance (Table. S1).

TEM analysis (Fig. 1 a) shows that the NCQDs have obvious quasi-spherical morphology and excellent water dispersion. The particle size analysis (Fig. 1b) shows that its particle sizes mainly range from 1 to 3 nm and the average diameter is about 1.84 nm, which is consistent with the nanoscale size of general carbon quantum dots [41]. The XRD pattern of NCQDs (Fig. 1c) shows that the obtained NCQDs have an obvious wide diffraction peak, suggesting that they have amorphous structure, which is consistent with the described characteristics of CQDs amorphous carbon nanoparticles [1,31,42].

In the FT-IR spectra of NCQDs (Fig. 1d), two obvious absorption peaks at 3343 cm−1 and 3120 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of OH or NH bonds[43]. The sharp peak at 1632 cm−1 is believed to be caused by the stretching vibration of CO bonds [27,44], and the absorption peak at 1319 cm−1 and 1213 cm−1 are presumed to be the bending vibration of OH and the stretching vibration of CO bonds[45], which fully indicates the existence of carboxyl functional groups. The absorption peaks at 1375 cm−1 and 977 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CN and the bending vibration of NH bonds [36,46], respectively, indicating that nitrogen has been successfully doped into the structure of carbon quantum dots.

The surface composition and chemical states of NCQDs were characterized by XPS measurements. As shown in Fig. 2 , there are obvious peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.1 eV and 531.0 eV, corresponding to carbon (71.47 %), nitrogen (12.64 %) and oxygen (15.90 %), respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b) shows four fitted peaks exhibited at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV, 288.2 eV, 288.9 eV, corresponding to the CC/CC, CO/CN, CO, O-CO bonds, respectively[25]. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2c) can be divided into three peaks at 399.2 eV, 400.2 eV, 401.8 eV, which would be contributed to the CN, N-C3, NH bonds[47]. Two peaks locate at 531.0 eV, 532.2 eV for NCQDs (Fig. 2d) are apportioned to CO and C-OH/COC bonds of O1s [42]. Therefore, there are many hydrophilic groups on NCQDs, which makes the products have excellent solubility and dispersion.

Fig. S2 and Fig. S3 show the 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra after mixing of raw materials. The large double peak at 1.361 ppm and the peak at 3.542–3.858 ppm are methyl and methylene structures in MGDA, and the peak in the range of 6.546–7.146 ppm is attributed to CH bonds in the benzene of MPD (Fig. S2)[48]. The peaks at 10.75 ppm and 63.72 ppm in 13C NMR spectra (Fig. S3) are attributed to –CH3 and –CH2- bonds in raw materials, while the peaks at 170.58 ppm and 173.56 ppm are caused by carbon in carboxyl group. Multiple peaks at 100–140 ppm are caused by carbon in aromatic ring. Fig. S4 and Fig. S5 show the 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra of the product NCQDs solution. The results show that while retaining the carbon peak and hydrogen peak contained in the raw materials, a large number of new peaks are generated, indicating that there are newly generated chemical groups, and nitrogen is successfully doped into the structure of carbon quantum dots. This is consistent with previous analysis of XPS and FT-IR.

As can be seen in Fig. 3 a, NCQDs have multiple different absorption peaks, suggesting a rich carbon skeleton structure. The smaller absorption peak at 240 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of conjugate CC bonds [49], while the obvious peak at 290 nm is due to the n-π* transition of CO in the carboxyl and carbonyl groups or CN bonds [14]. In addition, the wide absorption band between 400 nm and 460 nm is hypothesized to be caused by n-π* transitions on the surface of NCQDs [50]. The illustration in Fig. 3a shows that NCQDS solution fluoresces brilliant green under ultraviolet light and is yellow-green in direct sunlight, indicating that it has excellent fluorescence characteristics. The 3D-EEM scanning map (Fig. 3b) visually shows the fluorescence excitation and emission characteristics of carbon quantum dots. NCQDs has the optimal fluorescence emission wavelength at 510 nm at the excitation wavelength of 440 nm, and is independent of the change of excitation wavelength, which may be due to the effect of surface functional groups.

The Fig. 3c shows the stability evaluation of NCQDs in aqueous solution. Over-acidic or over-alkaline solution will lead to the decrease of the PL intensity (Fig. 3c), while it has a better performance under neutral conditions. Therefore, B-R buffer solution with pH = 7.0 ± 0.1 was selected as the sensing application environment. The fluorescence intensity of NCQDs remains stable in a high concentration of NaCl environment (Fig. 3d), and the fluorescence performance can still be developed after ultraviolet lamp irradiation for 100 min (Fig. 3e) and placed for 40 days (Fig. 3f), indicating that NCQDs have excellent salt resistance, photobleaching resistance and photostability. Subsequently, the concentration gradient of NCQDs was scanned by fluorescence emission spectrum (Fig. S6), and the results demonstrates that there was a quite excellent linear correlation between the concentration and fluorescence intensity, which further indicates the stability of their fluorescence characteristics and great potential in environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of NCQDs to Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Here, NCQDs are used as an indicator to detect mercury ions. Adding the same number of common ions to a 5 mg/L solution of NCQDs resulted in a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity only with mercury ions (Fig. S7, Fig. S8), which indicates that the NCQDs are potential selective fluorescent probes for mercury ions detection.

After varying the Hg2+ ions concentrations in the NCQDs solution, the fluorescence intensity at 510 nm was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 440 nm, and it was found that the emission peak intensity decreased significantly. The inset of Fig. 4 b shows that there is a strong linear correlation between fluorescence quenching rate (y, 1-F/F0) and Hg2+ ions concentration (x, 0–100 μM). The liner regression equation is y = 0.0051x-0.015 (R2 = 0.9943). The limit of detection (LOD) could be measured to be 0.9 μM according to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC)[51]. The interfering ions were then added to NCQDs that had quenched the fluorescence (Fig.4c), and the results show that the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs did not recover. This phenomenon suggests that interfering ions do not affect the detection process of Hg2+ ions, and the system of NCQDs is a selectively fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions. The comparison of different fluorescence sensing detection systems is shown in Table 1 . It should be noted that the NCQDs sensor has a much wider detection range for Hg2+ than previously reported and exhibits a higher fluorescence quantum yield. The results show that the sensor has a broad application prospect in environmental mercury detection.

The feasibility of fluorescence probes in actual water bodies determines their application prospect. To evaluate the applicability of NCQDs fluorescent probe in actual water samples, the standard addition method was used to quantify Hg2+ in two environmental water samples. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of the samples range from 99.64 % to 103.43 %, as well as the relative standard deviation (RSD) is lower than 5.34 %, indicating the effectiveness of the fluorescent probe system developed in this research for the detection of mercury ions in natural sources water environments, which offers a new scheme for the fluorescence detection of mercury ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of NCQDs by Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The principle of Hg2+ ions detection was analyzed. The fluorescence lifetime of the NCQDs solution before and after the addition of mercury ions was tested using transient fluorescence spectra. The results (Fig. 5 a) show that the fluorescence lifetimes of the two solutions are 5.3222 ns and 5.1895 ns, respectively, with no significant difference. The factor of dynamic quenching is preliminarily excluded [32]. The UV–vis absorption spectra of Hg2+ ions in Fig. 5b shows that it has no obvious absorption peak at 200–500 nm, and there is no overlap with the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of NCQDs, so it cannot form the internal filter effect (IFE) [28,57]. In addition, Fig. 5b shows that the absorption peaks at 290 nm and 210 nm disappear after the addition of Hg2+ ions, which is presumed to be caused by the generation of non-fluorescent groups caused by the static quenching effect [6]. The 13C NMR spectra analysis (Fig. 5c-d) shows that NCQDs have abundant surface groups, and these structures are the basis of their fluorescence characteristics. However, with the addition of excessive Hg2+ ions, 13C NMR spectra shows a dramatic change in the surface. The comparison of 13C NMR spectra confirmed the hypothesis that the quenching principle is static quenching.

The acute toxicity of NCQDs was studied in zebrafish. As shown in Table. S2, the concentration of NCQDs below 200 mg/L does not produce lethal effect on zebrafish, indicating that LC50＞100 mg/L. In practical application, the concentration of NCQDs is 5 mg/L, and the toxicity to aquatic organisms is very low. The results show that the detection system has low toxicity to water environment and is a kind of environment-friendly NCQDs with broad application prospects.

The project demonstrated the hydrothermal synthesis of NCQDs using MGDA and MPD as precursors. The product had high fluorescence quantum yield (63.80 %), uniform particle size (1–3 nm), bright green fluorescence (510 nm) at the excitation wavelength of 440 nm, showing good photostability. In the presence of Hg2+ ions, the fluorescence properties were lost due to static quenching to produce non-fluorescent groups, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was significantly quenched. The linear detection range of NCQDs for Hg2+ ions was 0–100 μM, and the LOD was 0.9 μM. The recovery experiments of tap water and river water verify the feasibility and reliability of the method, and the potential of NCQDs as ion sensor to detect Hg2+ ions in water samples was confirmed. The acute toxicity test (96 h) of the sensing system showed that the amount of NCQDs below 200 mg/L would not cause life damage to aquatic organisms (zebrafish as an example), so the conventional amount of NCQDs would cause little pollution to water environment. This work had good selectivity, wide detection range, good anti-interference performance and low toxicity to aquatic organisms. Overall, the environmentally friendly NCQDs fluorescent probe realizes the sensing application of heavy metal ions.

Guiqiao Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shurong Zhang: . Jinzhi Cui: Writing – review & editing. Wensu Gao: Writing – review & editing. Xing Rong: Writing – review & editing. Yaxin Lu: Writing – review & editing. Canzhu Gao: Project administration, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks for the support of experimental Instrument Platform of School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Utilization of waste biomass of Poa pratensis for green synthesis of n-doped carbon dots and its application in detection of Mn2+ and Fe3+
Chemosphere

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ir(III) complex-based phosphorescence and electrochemiluminescence chemodosimetric probes for Hg(Ⅱ) ions with high selectivity and sensitivity
Dalton Trans.

Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. He, Y. Wang, G. Mao, C. Liang, M. Fan, Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes based on carbon dots and multicolor CdTe quantum dots for multiplexed determination of heavy metal ions, Anal Chim Acta, 1191 (2022) 339251.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in nanoparticles based sensors for the detection of mercury (II) Ions in environmental and biological samples
Crit. Rev. Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Gul, S. Ullah, S. Khan, H. Ullah, M.U. Khan, M. Ullah, S. Ali, A.A. Altaf, Recent Progress in Nanoparticles Based Sensors for the Detection of Mercury (II) Ions in Environmental and Biological Samples, Crit Rev Anal Chem, (2022) 1-17.

J. Environ. Chem. Eng.

Ali, Recent developments in fluorescent and colorimetric chemosensors based on schiff bases for metallic cations detection: A review, Journal of Environmental Chemical Engineering, 9 (2021) 106381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from lotus root for Hg(II) ions detection and cell imaging
Appl. Surf. Sci.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective Hg (II) ion detection based on linear blue-shift of the maximum absorption wavelength of silver nanoparticles
J. Anal. Methods Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of B, N-doped CQDs as versatile fluorescence probes for sensitive detection of cobalt ions in environmental water and biological samples
Microchem. J.
